coredata.dc:title: High-performance supercapacitor electrode materials from composite of bamboo tar pitch activated carbon and tannic acid carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rong, Cheng
Liao, Chunxiao
Chen, Ying
Zheng, Xinyu
coredata.dc:description: A novel composite electrode material comprising bamboo tar pitch-based activated carbon (BTPAC) and tannic acid-derived carbon quantum dots (TACDs) was prepared. The integration of TACDs into the composite BTPAC-60/TACD not only preserved the high conductivity characteristic of BTPAC but also enhanced its hydrophilicity, pore structure, and specific surface area. Electrochemical evaluations revealed that BTPAC-60/TACD delivers a specific capacitance of 677.7 F/g at 0.5 A/g in a three-electrode system, and 281.8 F/g at 1 A/g in a two-electrode setup. Impressively, it achieves an energy density of 56.4 Wh/kg at a power density of 600 W/kg and maintains 95.7 % of its capacitance after 10,000 cycles. This research not only capitalizes on the high-value application of bamboo tar pitch but also provides a new perspective for the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bamboo tar pitch
Tannic acid
Activated carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BTPAC, TACDs and BTPAC-X/TACD
Characterization of BTPAC, TACDs and BTPAC-X/TACD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fujian Provincial Natural Science Foundation Scientific Research Fund (Grant 2019J01383 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Special Fund of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University (Grant KFB23079A ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A), (B), and (C) sequentially present the FTIR, Raman, and XRD spectra respectively, for BTPAC, BTPAC-X/TACD (with X ranging from 100 to 10), and TACDs, labeled from a to j. (D) illustrates the FESEM image of BTPAC.
(A) to (I) are TEM images of TACDs, BTPAC-X/TACD (X from 100 to 10) respectively.
(A), (C), (D) are the XPS full spectra of BTPAC, TACDs, and BTPAC-60/TACD, respectively, while (B), (D), and (E) are their corresponding C1s fitting spectra.
Pore size distribution curves of BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD composites.
Contact angles of BTPAC (A) and BTPAC-X/TACD (X ranging from 100 to 10, correspondingly labeled B through I) with water.
Comparative analysis of GCD (A) and specific capacitance (B) in BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD.
Detailed electrochemical evaluation of BTPAC-60/TACD. (A) showcases CV curves across diverse scan rates; (B) depicts GCD profiles at a range of current densities; (C) EIS Nyquist plots.

(A), (B), (C), and (D) represent the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), Ragone plot, and cyclic performance along with Coulombic efficiency, respectively, of BTPAC-60/TACD-SC; (E) and (F) depict SEM images of BTPAC-60/TACD electrode sheets before and after cycling; (G) illustrates BTPAC-60/TACD-SC practically illuminating an LED bulb.
Schematic diagram of preparation process of BTPAC-X/TACD composites.
Specific surface area and pore structure data of BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD composites.
Performance metrics of various supercapacitor electrode materials.

head.ce:title: High-performance supercapacitor electrode materials from composite of bamboo tar pitch activated carbon and tannic acid carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of JunCao Science and Ecology, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of JunCao Science and Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of JunCao Science and Ecology, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composite electrode material comprising bamboo tar pitch-based activated carbon (BTPAC) and tannic acid-derived carbon quantum dots (TACDs) was prepared. The integration of TACDs into the composite BTPAC-60/TACD not only preserved the high conductivity characteristic of BTPAC but also enhanced its hydrophilicity, pore structure, and specific surface area. Electrochemical evaluations revealed that BTPAC-60/TACD delivers a specific capacitance of 677.7 F/g at 0.5 A/g in a three-electrode system, and 281.8 F/g at 1 A/g in a two-electrode setup. Impressively, it achieves an energy density of 56.4 Wh/kg at a power density of 600 W/kg and maintains 95.7 % of its capacitance after 10,000 cycles. This research not only capitalizes on the high-value application of bamboo tar pitch but also provides a new perspective for the development of high-performance supercapacitor electrodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have become vital components in the global transition from traditional to renewable energy sources, driven by the increasing demand for sustainable energy solutions [1]. They are highly regarded in fields such as electric mobility, portable electronics, and renewable energy infrastructures due to their impressive power density, rapid charging and discharging capabilities, and long lifecycle [2]. The trend in supercapacitors is moving towards materials that provide higher energy densities, better power densities, and longer operational lifetimes [3]. This shift has spurred significant interest in environmentally friendly and cost-effective materials, including transition metal oxides and biochar [4–6]. Among the critical factors affecting supercapacitor performance, electrode materials play a pivotal role due to their influence on structural, surface, porosity, conductivity, and stability properties [7].

Carbon-based materials, such as activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and traditional charcoal, have been widely adopted in the field of supercapacitors [8]. These materials are preferred for their large surface areas, which offer numerous active sites for charge storage, enhancing their capacitive performance. Their inherent high electrical conductivity is essential for fast charge and discharge cycles, a crucial requirement for applications demanding instantaneous response. However, the high production costs and environmental concerns associated with non-renewable sources of these materials have prompted researchers to seek alternative solutions. This exploration has led to the development of biochar materials and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as sustainable and high-performance options for supercapacitors [9].

Biochar, derived from renewable biomass sources such as agricultural residues, forest byproducts, and municipal solid waste, is noted for its environmental compatibility and cost-effectiveness. During the carbonization process at high temperatures, biomass's inherent heteroatoms, like nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus, are integrated into the resulting biochar. The presence of these heteroatoms not only augments the wettability between the electrode surface and aqueous electrolytes but is also conjectured to bolster the pseudocapacitance of capacitors via redox reactions, thereby elevating the overall capacitance [10]. An exemplar of this is the bamboo-based biochar developed by Zhang, which exhibits remarkable rate capability, retaining a capacitance of 193.8 F/g even at an imposing current density of 20 A/g [11]. However, biochar materials face challenges such as variability in raw material sources, which can lead to significant fluctuations in quality and performance [12]. The chemical compositions and structures of various biomass sources necessitate careful selection and pre-treatment of raw materials to ensure consistency and predictability. Furthermore, the synthesis of biochar is sensitive to pyrolysis and activation conditions, with suboptimal processing potentially reducing electrochemical efficacy, thus requiring precise calibration of these parameters. Additionally, the limited energy density of these materials restricts their ability to store significant energy amounts [13].

Since the seminal discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by Xu et al. in 2004, CQDs have attracted significant attention as a novel genre of carbon material for supercapacitors [14]. CQDs are valued for their high electrical conductivity and large specific surface area, which facilitate rapid charge transfer and increased capacitance [15]. Their surfaces, rich in functional groups and heteroatom doping, enhance pseudocapacitance and electrolyte wettability [16]. Additionally, their easy synthesis and modifiability allow the creation of various CQD-based composites and architectures [17,18]. Nevertheless, employing CQDs in isolation as electrode materials is not without challenges, such as a lower energy density compared to other carbon materials like graphene and carbon nanotubes, attributable to limited pore volume and quantum size effects, and subpar cycling stability and rate performance under elevated current density or voltage due to the aggregation and dissolution of CQDs [17].

To address these issues, researchers have shifted towards developing composite material electrodes, which combine the advantages of multiple materials to achieve synergistic performance enhancements in supercapacitors. For instance, the composite material reported by Kumar, comprising PEG-400-derived carbon quantum dots integrated with activated carbon (AC/C-dots), demonstrated a 34 % surge in capacitance to 134 F/g, eclipsing the baseline capacitance of pure activated carbon at 100 F/g [19]. Similarly, the CQD/AC composite developed by Yu et al., combining lignin-based carbon with CQDs derived from citric acid and urea, achieved a capacitance increase from 125.8 F/g to 301.7 F/g, marking a 139.8 % improvement [20]. These studies highlight that innovative composite material designs can significantly enhance the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors.

In this context, our study explores a composite material made from bamboo tar pitch activated carbon (BTPAC) and tannic acid carbon quantum dots (TACDs). Bamboo tar pitch (BTP), a by-product of the pyrolytic distillation of bamboo processing residues, is rich in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and heteroatomic compounds, providing high carbon content and excellent chemical stability. These characteristics make it an ideal precursor for biomass-based activated carbon, offering a sustainable carbon source for supercapacitors. TACDs hold several potential advantages as supercapacitor electrode materials compared to other types of CQDs. Tannic acid, a naturally occurring biopolymer found in various plants, provides an abundant and readily available precursor, making TACDs more sustainable and cost-effective to produce compared to CQDs requiring complex synthesis processes. Additionally, tannic acid possesses a rich content of hydroxyl groups and phenolic groups, which can enhance the electrochemical performance of TACDs. These functional groups increase the number of electroactive sites for electrolyte ions, leading to greater charge storage capacity, and improve conductivity by promoting electron transfer within the TACDs. Moreover, the structure and properties of TACDs can be tailored by varying the synthesis conditions, allowing researchers to optimize them for specific supercapacitor applications. We integrated TACDs with BTPAC to create the BTPAC-X/TACD (X = 10, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 represents the mass ratio of BTPAC to tannic acid) composite material. The composite material combines the best of both worlds: the high surface area and remarkable porous architecture of BTPAC improve structural integrity and performance, while the inclusion of TACDs significantly boosts hydrophilicity, facilitating better interaction with aqueous environments. In electrochemical performance assays, the BTPAC-60/TACD composite material achieved an exceptional specific capacitance of 677.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g. This significant performance improvement underscores the synergistic effects of the composite's components. Such an inquiry opens up new opportunities for the high-value application of bamboo tar pitch and paves the way for designing and fabricating superior electrode materials for supercapacitors.

Bamboo tar pitch was obtained from Shuimu Haiqing Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Tannic acid, KOH, HCl, and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and acetylene black were acquired from Aladin Industrial Corporation Ltd. (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BTPAC, TACDs and BTPAC-X/TACD
ce:section.ce:para: Bamboo tar pitch powder was mixed with KOH in a 1:3 mass ratio and subjected to ball milling for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was exposed to heat treatment at 700 °C for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere in a tubular furnace resulting in the production of BTPAC. This product was then extensively washed with ultrapure water until it reached a neutral pH and subsequently dried for future applications.

TACDs were prepared via a microwave-assisted method. Specifically, 2 g of tannic acid was dissolved in 20 mL of water and subjected to microwave irradiation at 2.45 GHz for 20 min. The resulting black solid was dissolved, dialyzed in a 3500 Da membrane for 12 h to remove unreacted tannic acid, and freeze-dried.

To create BTPAC-X/TACD composites, BTPAC and tannic acid were mixed in mass ratios from 10:1 to 100:1. Each mixture was dispersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water, sonicated for 1 h, and microwaved at 2.45 GHz for 20 min. After that, the final BTPAC-X/TACD powders were obtained by dialyzing to remove any residual tannic acid and followed by freeze-drying. The preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 .

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses for all samples were conducted using an Avatar 360 Infrared Spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The spectral range spanned from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1, utilizing the potassium bromide (KBr) pellet technique. Raman spectroscopic analyses were performed on a LabRAM HR Evolution instrument (HORIBA Scientific, Japan) with a 532 nm laser, covering a range from 50 to 4000 cm−1. The surface morphology of BTPAC was examined via Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) using a TESCAN MIRA LMS instrument (TESCAN, Czech Republic) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to investigate the fine surface structures of TACDs and BTPAC-X/TACD. This analysis was conducted using a FEI Talos F200X microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Elemental composition and chemical states of TACDs, BTPAC, and BTPAC-60/TACD were meticulously analyzed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). This was performed on a K-Alpha instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al Kα radiation source (1486.6 eV) under a 400 μm diameter beam in an ultrahigh vacuum environment (<10−9 Torr). Specific surface area and pore structure assessments were conducted using an ASAP 2460 instrument (Micromeritics, USA), equipped with a degassing station. Prior to testing, samples were pretreated under vacuum at 120 °C for 8 h, followed by nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements at 77 K using liquid nitrogen. Surface hydrophilicity of the electrode sheets was evaluated using an SDC 350KS instrument (Kunshan Shengding, China) with deionized water as the medium.

To evaluate the electrochemical properties of BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD, electrochemical tests were conducted using a three-electrode system on a Shanghai Chenhua Electrochemical Workstation (CHI660E). Focusing on the working electrode, BTPAC-X/TACD, the preparation involved a meticulous process. Initially, BTPAC-X/TACD was blended with acetylene black and PTFE in an 8:1:1 mass ratio, followed by thorough mixing with ethanol to form a homogeneous paste. This mixture was then rolled out into a thin film, cut into 10 mm × 10 mm sections, and adhered to a 20 mm × 10 mm nickel foam substrate. After drying these assemblies at 120 °C for 2 h, they were compacted into electrodes using a press. In this setup, Hg/HgO and a platinum plate were utilized as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The entire electrode assembly was then immersed in a 6 mol/L KOH solution to carry out the electrochemical testing. The BTPAC-X/TACD composites were also fashioned into symmetric two-electrode systems encapsulated in a button-cell format. In this system, the electrodes were prepared in a manner similar to that of a three-electrode setup. Prior to encapsulation into button cells, the prepared electrodes and separator were immersed in 6 M KOH for 12 h.

Eqs. (1) to (4) detail the calculations for specific capacitance (F/g) in a three-electrode system, specific capacitance (F/g) in a two-electrode system, energy density (Wh/kg), and power density (W/kg), respectively [21]. Here, Cs represents the specific capacitance, m is the mass of active material in a single electrode, mt is the total mass of active materials in both electrodes, ΔV (V) denotes the potential range (excluding IR drop) during the electrode test in a symmetric two-electrode system, and Δt (s) refers to the discharge time.

C s
=
I · ∆ t

(2)

=
2 I · ∆ t

∆ t

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BTPAC, TACDs and BTPAC-X/TACD
ce:section.ce:para: Infrared spectroscopy reveals distinct differences between BTPAC and tannic TACDs (Fig. 1A). BTPAC exhibits few prominent vibrational peaks, indicating a lack of polar groups. In contrast, TACDs show numerous infrared features, highlighting their structural complexity. The broad peak at 3356 cm−1 corresponds to -OH stretching vibrations, indicating abundant hydroxyl groups. Peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1 are attributed to CO asymmetric stretching and CC stretching in aromatic rings, respectively, suggesting the presence of carboxylic acid groups and retained aromatic structures. Additional peaks at 1534 cm−1, 1446 cm−1, 1323 cm−1, 1203 cm−1, 1091 cm−1, and 1031 cm−1 correspond to various bending and stretching vibrations of CC, CH, and CO bonds, characteristic of phenolic compounds, secondary alcohols, or ethers. Weak peaks at 868.7 cm−1 and 756.5 cm−1 indicate out-of-plane bending vibrations of CH in benzene rings. As TACD content increases from BTPAC-100 to BTPAC-10, the intensity of these peaks rises, indicating a higher concentration of polar groups on the BTPAC surface, correlating with significant changes in the physicochemical properties and functionality of BTPAC materials.

Raman spectroscopy is a pivotal tool for elucidating the structural characteristics of carbon materials, effectively distinguishing between graphitic and amorphous carbon. As shown in Fig. 1B, the Raman spectra of BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD samples reveal the G band of graphitic carbon at 1569 cm−1 and the D band, indicative of amorphous carbon, at 1338 cm−1. Notably, while the D band position for TACDs aligns with that of BTPAC, its G band exhibits a red shift to 1597 cm−1, indicating a higher degree of graphitic ordering. The intensity ratio of the G peak to the D peak (IG/ID) serves as an indicator of the graphitization level of the materials, with a higher IG/ID value signifying greater graphitization and a lower value indicating enhanced disorder. TACDs demonstrate an IG/ID ratio of 1.35, significantly higher than that of the other samples, corroborating its heightened graphitization extent. In fact, TEM observations (Fig. 2A) further substantiate this, vividly illustrating the presence of graphite lattice fringes in TACDs. A detailed examination of the IG/ID ratios for BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD (with X varying from 100 to 10) reveals values of 1.07, 1.10, 1.12, 1.18, 1.21, 1.19, 1.17, 1.14, and 1.13, respectively. This analysis indicates a trend where the IG/ID ratio initially increases before declining with the addition of TACDs, peaking at 1.21 for BTPAC-60/TACD. This finding underscores that a moderate inclusion of TACDs can enhance the graphitization level of the composite material, a critical factor in improving its electrical conductivity. However, beyond a certain threshold, further increases in TACDs content do not enhance the graphitization level. Instead, the aggregation of TACDs on the material's surface introduces local structural disorder, as visually corroborated by TEM imagery (Fig. 2). The slight resurgence of disorder, reflected in a marginal dip in the IG/ID ratio, highlights the necessity of maintaining an optimal TACDs content to prevent structural degradation. Consequently, BTPAC-60/TACD emerges as an exemplary composition, optimally balancing graphitization enhancement and structural order.

During the XRD analysis (Fig. 1C), TACDs exhibited a broad peak at 23°, which correlates to the diffraction feature of the (002) plane of graphite, albeit with a noticeable shift [22]. This broad peak aligns with the Scherrer formula's description of peak broadening due to small crystal size [23], indicating the presence of nanoscale crystalline domains within TACDs, as further substantiated by TEM observations. Notably, the XRD pattern of TACDs lacks the characteristic (100) plane diffraction peak typically observed around 44° in conventional graphite [22]. This absence is attributed to the minute size of TACDs (approximately 3 nm), causing the (100) plane peak to broaden excessively and merge into the background. In contrast, BTPAC displays a more pronounced diffraction peak near 44°, indicative of larger graphite crystalline regions. Analyzing the transition from BTPAC-10/TACD to BTPAC-100/TACD, a relative stability in peak intensity near 23° is observed, while the intensities of peaks around 44° are notably attenuated, increasingly resembling the characteristics of TACDs. This attenuation becomes more pronounced with higher loadings of TACDs. This trend not only illustrates the significant impact of TACDs' ultramicroscopic dimensions on the diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of the composite materials but also indirectly confirms the successful attachment of TACDs onto the BTPAC surface, effectively modifying the surface structural properties of BTPAC.

In the FESEM analysis of BTPAC, the surface exhibited a distinctly rugged texture with numerous crater-like pores, evoking images of a lunar landscape (Fig. 1D). The distinctive texture primarily arises from the reaction between KOH, serving as a chemical activator, and the carbon compounds in bamboo tar pitch at high temperatures. This interaction fundamentally alters the structure of the carbon material and promotes the development of porosity through the release of gases such as carbon dioxide and water vapor, thereby enhancing the material's porosity. Furthermore, the carbonization phase contributes to this porosity through the decomposition of organic compounds within the bamboo tar pitch, releasing gases that form additional cavities [24]. These structural alterations significantly increase the specific surface area and the number of active sites, which are crucial for enhancing the performance of supercapacitors.

The true nature of the TACDs synthesized was vividly revealed under HRTEM analysis (Fig. 2A). Displaying a size of around 3 nm, these TACDs possessed clear lattice fringes. The measured lattice spacing of 0.22 nm aligns with the (100) planes of graphite, suggesting that the TACDs are essentially graphitized carbon quantum dots. Further investigation into the distribution of TACDs on the surface of BTPAC-X revealed a strong correlation between the uniformity of TACDs dispersion and the proportion of tannic acid in the synthesis feedstock. In the BTPAC-100/TACD sample, a sparse population of TACDs was observed (Fig. 2B), consistent with tannic acid comprising only 1 % of the total BTP mass in the formulation design. As the tannic acid fraction increased to 1/60, there was a marked increase in the surface density of TACDs on BTPAC, with excellent dispersibility maintained within this compositional range (Fig. 2E). However, exceeding the 1/60 threshold led to a reduction in the average inter-particle spacing of TACDs on the BTPAC surface (Fig. 2F–I), indicating the onset of agglomeration. This trend towards aggregation was most pronounced in the BTPAC-10/TACD specimen (Fig. 2I), where the degree of TACDs aggregation reached its maximum. Based on these observations, BTPAC-60/TACD demonstrated the optimal distribution characteristics of TACDs, characterized by a high particle count while maintaining adequate dispersibility. This state is crucial for maximizing the composite's performance attributes.

To gain a profound understanding of the effects of TACDs introduction on the surface functional groups of BTPAC, we conducted a meticulous analysis of the full-spectrum XPS and C 1s peak fitting spectra for BTPAC, TACDs, and the representative composite BTPAC-60/TACD – a material that has repeatedly emerged with distinctive characteristics in prior assessments (Fig. 3 ). The results indicate that BTPAC exhibits the highest carbon content at 83.53 % (Fig. 3A), while TACDs and BTPAC-60/TACD show comparable carbon contents of 72.69 % (Fig. 3C) and 72.93 % (Fig. 3D), respectively. Notably, the oxygen content in BTPAC is only 13.14 %, significantly lower than the 20.84 % observed in TACDs. This disparity aligns with expectations, as BTPAC undergoes high-temperature treatment leading to the degradation of surface oxygen-containing groups, a fact also verified by the absence of specific oxygen group vibration peaks in the infrared spectrum. Interestingly, the oxygen content in BTPAC-60/TACD rises to 23.99 %, highlighting the efficacy of TACDs in increasing the oxygen content on BTPAC's surface. This increase may also be attributed to the polar groups of TACDs enhancing the adsorption of environmental water molecules, thereby reflecting a higher oxygen content in the measurement. Examining their C 1s fitting spectra (Fig. 3B, D, F), all three samples display characteristic peaks for CO and CO near 286 eV and 288 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the evolution of the C 1s peak profile in BTPAC-60/TACD suggests a transition from BTPAC-like to TACDs-like characteristics, indirectly validating the effective modification of BTPAC's surface structure following TACDs incorporation. Collectively, these findings underscore the modulatory role of TACDs on the surface properties of BTPAC.

As illustrated in Table 1 , the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the samples progressively increases from 773.962 m2/g for BTPAC to 799.066 m2/g for BTPAC-60/TACD, followed by a slight decline to 776.028 m2/g with higher TACDs content. The total pore volume shows a gradual increment with the addition of TACDs, rising from 0.328 cm3/g for BTPAC to 0.439 cm3/g for BTPAC-60/TACD, and then dropping to 0.330 cm3/g with further increase in TACDs content. While the micropore surface area remains relatively constant across all samples, the mesopore surface area increases with the rise in TACD content, peaking at 18.358 m2/g in BTPAC-60/TACD, suggesting that TACDs facilitate the formation of mesoporous structures. The micropore volume shows minimal variation among the samples, whereas the mesopore volume significantly escalates with the increment of TACDs, reaching a maximum of 0.121 cm3/g in BTPAC-60/TACD, consistent with the increase in mesopore surface area. The mean pore diameter ranges from 1.695 nm to 1.708 nm across all specimens, indicating that the addition of TACDs does not fundamentally alter the basic pore structure characteristics of BTPAC but rather adjusts the ratio between micropores and mesopores. Furthermore, Fig. 4 illustrates that both BTPAC and BTPAC-X/TACD exhibit high pore volumes in the microporous range (<2 nm), with overlapping curves, signifying a similar pore architecture in this regime. The introduction of TACDs generates a peak at approximately 4 nm for BTPAC-X/TACD, which intensifies with increased TACDs loading, peaks in BTPAC-60/TACD, and subsequently diminishes. Collectively, the incorporation of TACDs optimizes the porosity of BTPAC, with BTPAC-60/TACD achieving a relatively optimal surface property profile, particularly regarding the augmented mesoporosity that facilitates electrolyte infiltration and ion transport rates. These properties are crucial for applications such as supercapacitors, which demand rapid charge-discharge cycles.

Electrode films made from BTPAC and various BTPAC-X/TACD composites were tested for water droplet contact angles to assess their hydrophilicity. As shown in Fig. 5 , the BTPAC material exhibits the highest contact angle at 129.858°, indicating its low hydrophilicity. With increasing TACDs content from BTPAC-100/TACD to BTPAC-10/TACD, the contact angle progressively decreases from 124.366° to 85.675°. This substantial reduction clearly demonstrates that the electrode materials become more hydrophilic with higher TACDs content. This change is directly attributed to the increase in polar, oxygen-bearing functional groups on the surface of TACDs, which enhance the interaction between the material surface and water molecules, thereby improving the overall hydrophilicity of the material.

In our quest to discern the impact of varying TACDs content on the electrochemical attributes of BTPAC-X/TACD composites, we embarked on a comparative analysis against the non-composite BTPAC. As delineated in Fig. S1, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles of both BTPAC-X/TACD and BTPAC manifest remarkably smooth curves and conforming closely to a near-rectangular shape. This observation underscores the adherence of their charge transfer processes to the ideal double-layer charging mechanism, a hallmark of efficient electrochemical behavior. The CV curves' integral areas serving as proxies for capacitance reveal a compelling narrative: BTPAC, with the smallest CV area, signifies a lower capacitance, thereby underscoring the capacitive enhancement imbued by the TACDs integration.

Further elucidation of their charge-discharge dynamics was sought through GCD testing, as depicted in Fig. 6A. Here, BTPAC's GCD curve exhibiting a pronounced triangular symmetry reaffirms its identity as a classic electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC). Contrastingly, the BTPAC-X/TACD composites, despite maintaining a broadly triangular symmetry, reveal subtle curve distortions. These distortions hint at underlying polarization effects, especially pronounced at the beginning of charging and discharging phases, where ion aggregation on the electrode surface leads to a disproportionate charge distribution between the electrolyte ions and the electrode material. This discrepancy shifts the potential distribution across the electrode surface, thereby influencing the charge-discharge curve's shape. In the arena of specific capacitance, as demonstrated in Fig. 6B, BTPAC stands at the lower echelon with a capacitance of 167.9 F/g at 1 A/g. In an intriguing twist, the specific capacitance of BTPAC-X/TACD shows a fascinating pattern: it initially increases, then decreases, as the BTPAC content rises (X from 10 to 100), reaching its peak at X = 60 where BTPAC-60/TACD achieves a zenith of 326.1 F/g. This pattern unveils a delicate equilibrium. At X = 10, the capacitance is hindered by the high TACD content, which potentially obstructs BTPAC's pores, limiting the enhancement of capacitance. Conversely, when X surpasses 60, the reduced proportion of TACD restricts the overall capacitance gain. Consequently, subsequent studies focused primarily on BTPAC-60/TACD as the material of interest.

In the CV analysis of BTPAC-60/TACD conducted at varying scan rates (Fig. 7A), it was observed that the CV curve maintained a quasi-rectangular shape as the scan rate increased from 40 mV/s to 200 mV/s, indicating that the material sustains its capacitive performance even at higher charge-discharge rates, without significant loss in capacitance or a drop in voltage. Such a characteristic is typically indicative of low internal resistance, suggesting that the electrode material can swiftly respond to changes in voltage and that ions within the electrolyte can quickly diffuse to the active sites on the electrode. However, as the scan rate was further increased to 280 mV/s, the CV curve began to transition into a tilted olive-shaped form. This change signifies that BTPAC-60/TACD has reached a diffusion-limited state. At this juncture, further increases in scan rate leads to an escalation in internal resistance and a rise in voltage drop, culminating in a reduced energy storage capability. This trend underscores the material's efficiency in rapid charge-discharge cycles up to a certain threshold, beyond which the diffusion limitations and increased internal resistance start to impact its electrochemical performance negatively.

In our investigation, detailed GCD tests of BTPAC-60/TACD were conducted across a spectrum of current densities, ranging from 0.5 to 16 A/g, as depicted in Fig. 7B. At a modest current density of 0.5 A/g, the material showcases an outstanding specific capacitance of 677.7 F/g, highlighting its exceptional energy storage capabilities—qualities that are critically sought after for applications requiring extensive capacity and prolonged energy discharge. Remarkably, even as the current density increases to 16 A/g, BTPAC-60/TACD demonstrates a notable specific capacitance of 180.3 F/g, evidencing its strong rate performance and resilience under higher power demands.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is utilized to gauge charge transfer and ion diffusion resistances within electrochemical systems, applying a sinusoidal potential to measure current responses and plotting complex impedance against frequency in a Nyquist plot, thereby revealing insights into the system's charge transfer and ion diffusion behaviors [25]. Typically, the semicircle on the Nyquist plot symbolizes charge transfer resistance, where a smaller radius indicates lower impedance, and the straight line represents ion diffusion resistance, with a steeper slope denoting quicker diffusion and improved capacitive attributes. In our comprehensive EIS analysis of BTPAC-60/TACD, we noted a significantly steeper slope in the Nyquist plot's linear segment compared to pure BTPAC (Fig. 7C), evidencing enhanced ion diffusion rates post TACDs integration. This enhancement is largely due to the increased presence of surface polar groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, introduced by TACDs. These additions boost the composite's hydrophilicity, improving ion adsorption and diffusion at the electrode interface. Moreover, TACDs not only augment the specific surface area, providing more active sites for ion storage, but also optimize the porous structure by increasing both the total pore volume and the proportion of mesopores—key factors for efficient ion transport. Collectively, these structural and chemical modifications by TACDs enhance electrolyte ion-electrode interactions and establish more effective ion pathways, significantly accelerating ion diffusion rates. Additionally, the comparison of Nyquist plots for both BTPAC-60/TACD and BTPAC reveals that the diameters of their respective semicircular portions are roughly equivalent. This observation suggests that the integration of TACDs with BTPAC does not diminish the electrical conductivity of BTPAC. In essence, BTPAC-60/TACD amalgamates the high electrical conductivity inherent in BTPAC with the enhanced hydrophilicity provided by TACDs. This synergy results in a composite that not only maintains the efficient charge transfer characteristics of BTPAC but also benefits from the improved ion interaction at the electrode interface due to TACDs. Consequently, the capacitive performance of BTPAC-60/TACD is significantly enhanced, leveraging the combined advantages of both components.

To gain insights into the real-world application potential and performance of BTPAC-60/TACD, a symmetrical two-electrode supercapacitor, designated as BTPAC-60/TACD-SC, was meticulously assembled using a button cell format. The voltage window of a supercapacitor is pivotal, as it establishes the upper limit of operational voltage that can be applied without risking electrolyte decomposition or damage to the electrode materials, thereby profoundly influencing the energy density. As the voltage window for BTPAC-60/TACD-SC gradually extends from 0–1 V to 0–1.4 V, an associated increase in polarization is discernible within its CV curves, as illustrated in Fig. S2. Reaching a voltage of 1.4 V, the curve displays marked polarization, highlighting enhanced electrolyte decomposition at this juncture. Despite the slight polarization observed at 1.2 V, this level is considered to have a net positive impact on TBTPAC-60/TACD-SC's overall performance. Consequently, this pattern suggests that although higher voltages contribute to increased polarization, a careful elevation to 1.2 V represents an optimal compromise that enhances BTPAC-60/TACD-SC's functionality without compromising its stability. Cyclic voltammetry analysis of TBTPAC-60/TACD-SC conducted in the 0–1.2 V range shows that at a low scan rate of 50 mV/s, the CV curve closely approximates a rectangular profile, albeit with slight deviations in the 0–0.2 V and 1–1.2 V segments during charging and discharging cycles (Fig. 8A). With an increase in scan rate, these deviations from the rectangular profile are accentuated and span a wider voltage spectrum. The lack of discernible redox peaks on the CV curve suggests that these discrepancies are largely due to diffusion kinetic constraints of electrolyte ions at the TBTPAC-60/TACD interface. GCD assessments demonstrate that BTPAC-60/TACD-SC's GCD profiles are symmetrically triangular, signifying effective charge and discharge cycles (Fig. 8B). At a current density of 1 A/g, it records a specific capacitance of 281.8 F/g. Significantly, even at an elevated current density of 16 A/g, the device retains 33.6 % of its initial capacitance. Such results accentuate the exceptional energy storage prowess of BTPAC-60/TACD-SC at lower current densities and its admirable capacity retention under higher operational demands, showcasing its durability and performance in energy storage technologies.

Energy and power densities are key metrics for assessing supercapacitor performance. Our comprehensive evaluation of BTPAC-60/TACD-SC reveals that this device offers a substantial energy density of 56.4 Wh/kg at a lower power density of 600 W/kg, as depicted in Fig. 8C. As the power demand increases, BTPAC-60/TACD-SC experiences a modest reduction in energy density. Importantly, it maintains an energy density of 18.9 Wh/kg even under a high power density of 9603 W/kg.

The cycling stability of the capacitor was benchmarked against the initial specific capacitance obtained from the first charge-discharge cycle (Fig. 8D). During the initial 600 cycles, capacitance retention stabilized at 100 %. Subsequently, between cycles 700 and 3900, this ratio notably increased to 110 %, momentarily dipping to 100 % during the 2500th and 3200th cycles. From cycles 4000 to 7500, capacitance retention exhibited a fluctuating trend, oscillating between 102.9 % and 114.1 %. From cycle 7600 to the conclusion at 10000 cycles, this ratio gradually decreased from 104.1 % to 95.7 %. Regarding Coulombic efficiency, it remained stable during the first 1700 cycles, predominantly achieving 100 %. However, from cycle 1800 to 7400, Coulombic efficiency experienced substantial fluctuations, ranging from 92.8 % to 104.8 %. In the later cycles, from 7400 to 10,000, the amplitude of fluctuations narrowed, settling between 94.4 % and 101.2 %, with the majority of instances maintaining a high efficiency level between 97 % and 98 %. These cycling characteristics illustrate that the BTPAC-60/TACD-SC initially experiences a performance boost, followed by mid-term fluctuations, and a gradual decline during prolonged use. Despite the aging process, it sustains a relatively high Coulombic efficiency, testifying to a commendable stability profile.

Fig. 8E and F depict SEM images of BTPAC-60/TACD electrode sheets before cycling and after undergoing 10,000 cycles, respectively. Remarkably, even after 10,000 cycles, the electrode's primary architecture remains largely intact, with only minor surface loosening observed. This slight alteration is likely due to the deep permeation of the electrolyte and the dynamic processes of reactant insertion and extraction that occur repeatedly during cycling. Importantly, these cumulative changes do not cause significant damage to the electrode's fundamental structure, underscoring the exceptional chemical stability of BTPAC-60/TACD and its resilience under prolonged cyclic charging and discharging conditions. Table 2 presents a comparative analysis of specific capacitance, energy density, power density, and cycling stability among a range of biomass-derived carbon materials and carbon quantum dot-enhanced carbon materials. The data clearly indicates that BTPAC-60/TACD demonstrates an electrochemical performance that is comparable to, and potentially surpasses, the benchmarks reported in existing literature. When two button cells prepared using the aforementioned method were connected in series, they successfully sustained the illumination of an LED bulb for 12 min, thereby demonstrating the potential practical applicability of BTPAC-60/TACD (Fig. 8G).

The innovative BTPAC-60/TACD composite electrode material, combining bamboo tar pitch-based activated carbon (BTPAC) with tannic acid carbon quantum dots (TACDs), was developed using a microwave method. Leveraging the synergistic effects between BTPAC and TACDs, BTPAC-60/TACD successfully merges the high conductivity of BTPAC with the enhanced hydrophilicity, hierarchical pore structure, and elevated specific surface area imparted by TACDs. These attributes facilitate the ingress and egress of electrolyte ions, laying the groundwork for exceptional supercapacitive behavior. In a three-electrode system, BTPAC-60/TACD exhibits an outstanding specific capacitance of 677.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g, and in a two-electrode configuration, it achieves 281.8 F/g at 1 A/g. Additionally, the BTPAC-60/TACD-based supercapacitor (BTPAC-60/TACD-SC) demonstrates an energy density of 56.4 Wh/kg at a power density of 600 W/kg and maintains 18.9 Wh/kg at an elevated power density of 9603 W/kg, alongside impressive cycle stability. This research not only provides a sustainable use for bamboo tar pitch but also sets a new precedent in designing advanced supercapacitor electrodes.

This work was supported by the Fujian Provincial Natural Science Foundation Scientific Research Fund (Grant 2019J01383) and the Science and Technology Innovation Special Fund of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University (Grant KFB23079A).

Cheng Rong: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chunxiao Liao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ying Chen: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xinyu Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2024.112657.
